title: A modified CTAB method for the simultaneus extraction of host and pathogen total RNA in inoculated conifer plants
author: Clarissa Lopez Del Visco

steps_raw.instruction: Assuming that sampling has been done and that tissue collected has been flash frozen by immersion in liquid nitrogen (LN2), and stored in a -80ºC fridge, this first step requires a mortar and pestle as well as liquid nitrogen L2N.
Take samples from the -80ºC and place them on ice or a cool rack.
Inside the chemical hood, add beta-mercaptoethanol 2.67% (20 μL) to each sample.
Before starting: sterilize mortar and pestle in autoclave. We recommend working in batches of 12 or 24 samples so you will need an equal amount of mortar and pestles.
Add 750 μL of warm CTAB extraction buffer to each sample.
Place the frozen samples's tissue in mortar and pour LN2 to make the sample bubble, careful to not cause tissue to jump out of the mortar.
Use the pestle to grind the sample. It is recommended to keep grinding the sample until a very fine powder is obtained. If needed, gradually add LN2 while grinding to maintain low temperature.

Put the powder in a new sterile vial. A screw cap vial, such as Falcon 15mL tubes is recommended, because microcentrifuge tube cap may suddenly pop when re-exposed to room temperature after staying in very low temperatures. NOTE: Glass tubes are also challenging because ink, tape, or stickers don't work at very low temperatures. Store the ground samples in -80ºC fridge.

NOTE: because of the overnight precipitation, it is recommended to start this process in the afternoon. Before starting: preheat a water bath and a dry thermal bath, both at 65ºC. Heat the CTAB extraction buffer at 65ºC.
Mix by inverting the tubes, ensuring that the buffer thoroughly makes contact with all of the powdered tissue. Do not shake.
Incubate samples in dry thermal bath for 10 minutes; mix by inverting tubes after 5 minutes. Do not shake.
Perform the first washing. Bring the samples back to the chemical hood. Add 750 μL of Chloroform:Isoamil alchohol (24:1) (CHISAM) to each sample.
Mix by inverting the tubes. Do not shake.

Centrifuge at 4,500 rpm for 20 minutes at room temperature. It is preferable to place centrifuge in chemical hood for the remainder of the extraction process. Prepare new set of 2mL microcentrifuge tubes.

Collect the supernatant and transfer into the new 2mL microcentrifuge tubes. At this stage, it's not a problem if some plant material ends up with the collected supernatant. Dispose of the remaining liquid.
Perform the second washing. Add 750 μL of Chloroform:Isoamil alchohol (24:1) (CHISAM) to each sample.
Centrifuge at 4,500 rpm for 20 minutes at room temperature. Prepare new set of 2mL microcentrifuge tubes.
Collect the supernatant and transfer into the new 2mL microcentrifuge tubes. Again, dispose of the remaining liquid.
To each sample, add 1/4 volume of Lithium Chloride (LiCl) 10M.
Place each tube in a styrofoam box filled with ice.
Place the box in a 4ºC fridge or a 4ºC cold room, and leave samples to decant overnight.
Before starting: 1) Preheat a water bath and a heat block at 60ºC. 2) Heat the SSTE buffer at 60ºC. 3) Cool the centrifuge at -4ºC.
Centrifuge samples at 10,000 rpm for 30 minutes at -4ºC.

Add 700 μL of warm SSTE buffer. If needed, pipet to mix the buffer inside the tube and re-suspend the pellet by disturbing it. At this stage, the pellet may be a dark visible brown or a very faint, clear smudge. It is not uncommon to not see the pellet at all.
Perform the third washing. Bring the samples back to the chemical hood. Add 700 μL of CHISAM to each sample.
Incubate samples at 60ºC for 5-10 minutes to re-suspend the pellet.
Mix by inverting the tubes.
Centrifuge at 4,500 rpm for 10 minutes at room temperature. Prepare new 2mL microcentrifuge tubes.
Collect the supernatant and transfer into the new 2mL microcentrifuge tubes. Dispose of the remaining liquid.
Perform the fourth washing. Add 700 μL of CHISAM to each sample.
Centrifuge at 4,500 rpm for 10 minutes at room temperature. Prepare new set of 2mL microcentrifuge tubes.
Collect the supernatant and transfer into the new 2mL microcentrifuge tubes. Dispose of the remaining liquid.
Add 2 volumes of cold absolute ethanol.
Incubate at -80ºC for 1 hour. NOTE: now is a convenient time to prepare the gel for imaging the extracted product. In the meantime, cool down the centrifuge to -4ºC.
Centrifuge samples at 13,000 rpm for 30 minutes at -4ºC.

When the pellet is dry, add 25-50 μL of DEPC H 2 O. Note: The amount of water used to elute the samples depends upon the conectration of RNA you expect to obtain. It is recommended to have at least 100 ng/μL to start preparing the library.

Carefully discard the supernatant and place tubes in ice or cold rack. Tip: avoid tilting the tube too quickly to avoid disturbing the pellet; avoid tilting more than 90º. At this stage, the pellet should appear as a small clear or white smudge.
Wash with 700 μL of cold 70% ethanol.
Centrifuge samples at 10,000 rpm for 2 minutes at -4ºC
Discard the supernatant and place tubes in ice or cold rack.
Centrifuge samples at 10,000 rpm for 2 minutes ar -4ºC
Discard the supernatant and let the tubes air dry in the hood. You can use P10 pipette to gently remove the excess ethanol to advance the drying stage.
Store samples in -80ºC

It is recommended have a rough estimate of the concentrations and quality by measuring with Nanodrop. For more accurate quantifications, use Qubit. It is recommended to make gel images for each extracted samples. Before proceeding with library preparation, it is advisable to measure RNA integrity with bioanalyzer. This is a very lengthy extraction and takes some time to master. Begin with extracting a small sets of just a few samples to familiarize witht his procedure before increasing to sets of 24 samples.
Remove samples and place them in a styrofoam box filled with ice.

Note: This last step explain a cleanup procedure using enzimatic DNA digestion and beads. Make sure to bring SPRI beads to room temperature; vortex thoroughly to ensure that the breads are fully resuspended.

Dilute your samples to a concentration of 16.6μL in 100μL volume. This amount can be adjusted accordingly with the RNA yield that you have obtained. We recommend to have > 1000 ng of total RNA in 100μL before starting with the clean up.
Add 1μL DNAse of your choice and 10μL of 10x buffer from your DNAse kit.
Incubate at 37º for 10 minutes.
Add 200μL (2x volume) of SPRI beads. Mix on touch vortex and spin down.
Incubate at room temperature for at least 5 minutes to maximize recovery.

Carefully place samples on a magnet rack for 5 minutes or until the beads have been fully collected on the tube wall and the solution is visibly clear. You may notice that some beads remain fixed at the bottom of the tube. We recommend gently moving the tube up and down to guide all the beads to the magnet point at the side of the tube.
Remove all supernatant, making sure not to touch the beads. Dispose of the supernatant.
Add 200μL of freshly prepared 80% ETOH. Again, take care not to disturb the beads.
Incubate at room temperature for 2 minutes without removing the tubes from the magnet or disturbing the beads.
Carefully remove and discard the ETOH.
Repeat steps 15.8 to 15.10 for a total of 2 washes.
Allow remaining ETOH to evaporate by air drying the beads for 3-5 minutes. The beads are sufficiently dry when cracks begin to appear in the bead pellet and no visible liquid remains.
Re-suspend beads in 32μL.
Incubate at room temperature for 2 minutes.
Place samples on magnet until supernatant is clear. Again, you may need to guide remaining beads at the bottom to join at the magnet on the side of the tube.
Transfer 30μL of supernatant to new tube.
At this point the RNA should be purified and ready to be used for library prep.
